question: How should we build an Android app that emulates a console such as the Game Boy Advance, using academic and open-source evidence?

items.claim: Reusing an existing emulator core is the lowest-risk route to a usable Android GBA app.
items.conditions: Repository metadata and source trees retrieved 2026-07-09; no local build performed.
items.limits: Does not compare measured performance on the target Android devices.

items.claim: SkyEmu is the shortest permissively licensed fork path because it is MIT-licensed and contains an Android Gradle project and Android deployment workflow.
items.limits: Its native build is described by the project as experimental; UX and device compatibility still require validation.

items.claim: mGBA is the strongest candidate for a custom product core, but the canonical repository does not provide a first-party Android frontend.
items.conditions: mGBA is MPL-2.0 and provides C/C++, CMake, a libretro core, core tests, fuzz/performance tools, and save-state features.
items.limits: Android NDK integration and MPL compliance need product-specific engineering/legal review.

items.claim: Lemuroid is the best Android-native reference implementation for a libretro-based emulator frontend.
items.conditions: The project identifies Android integration and ease of use as goals and uses mGBA for GBA.
items.limits: Lemuroid and LibretroDroid are GPL-3.0; this may be unsuitable for a closed-source app.

items.claim: A libretro boundary separates the emulator core from frontend audio, video, input, and lifecycle concerns.
items.conditions: RetroArch README describes cores as dynamic libraries using generic audio/video/input callbacks.
items.limits: The reference frontend and common Android host libraries reviewed here are GPL-3.0.

items.claim: Android GameActivity is suitable when most emulator runtime code is C/C++.
items.conditions: Android documentation says GameActivity passes lifecycle, input, and text input to native C/C++ code.
items.limits: A Kotlin UI shell may still prefer a conventional Activity plus JNI/SurfaceView.

items.claim: Low-latency Android audio should use Oboe/AAudio, low-latency mode, callbacks, the natural sample rate, and no blocking in callbacks.
items.type: official platform guidance plus academic paper
items.conditions: Android guidance retrieved 2026-07-09; Balsini et al., Journal of Systems and Software, 2019.
items.limits: End-to-end latency must be measured on the target device matrix.

items.claim: Android frame presentation needs explicit pacing rather than an unconstrained render loop.
items.conditions: The Android Frame Pacing library targets smooth OpenGL/Vulkan rendering and correct frame pacing.
items.limits: A GBA core runs near 59.73 Hz, so emulator-specific audio/video synchronization still needs measurement.

items.claim: Controller support should classify Android input sources and map buttons, D-pad, and analog axes separately.
items.conditions: Android documents SOURCE_GAMEPAD, SOURCE_DPAD, SOURCE_JOYSTICK, KeyEvent, and MotionEvent handling.
items.limits: Controller mapping quirks require physical-device testing.

items.claim: ROM selection should use Android's Storage Access Framework and retain user-granted document access rather than broad storage permission.
items.conditions: ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT is appropriate for long-term access to a user-selected document.
items.limits: Native cores may require an fd bridge or a private cached copy.

items.claim: Cycle-accurate emulation and dynamic binary translation are feasible, but neither should be an MVP requirement without profiling.
items.conditions: Richie and Ross, MEMOCODE 2014, studied cycle-accurate 8080 emulation on ARM11 with DBT; Jebali and Potop-Butucaru, ACSD 2018, studied consistency between cycle-accurate and instruction-set simulators.
items.limits: Neither study evaluates a GBA emulator on modern Android hardware.

items.claim: GBA correctness should be judged with conformance ROMs and difficult commercial titles, not only frame rate.
items.conditions: SkyEmu and NanoBoyAdvance self-report passing multiple suites; test repositories were checked for license and activity.
items.limits: Pass claims were not independently reproduced; armwrestler-gba-fixed had no detected license.

items.claim: Save states need atomic, versioned persistence tied to ROM and core identity.
items.type: academic transfer evidence plus OSS feature evidence
items.conditions: de Winkel et al., IMWUT 2020, demonstrate selective state tracking for power-failure-resilient Game Boy emulation; mGBA provides save states.
items.limits: The paper targets intermittent battery-free hardware, not Android lifecycle semantics.

items.claim: Distribution must avoid unlicensed ROMs, BIOS files, trademarks, screenshots, and cover art.
items.conditions: Google Play disallows IP infringement; scholarship documents copyright and preservation tensions.
items.confidence: high for store-policy risk; low for jurisdiction-specific legality
items.limits: Not legal advice; counsel must review target markets and product behavior.
